concurrency_note: ci_scaffold's detect_ci is a static filesystem presence check at a point in time (does .github/workflows/ hold any *.yml/*.yaml file); there is no concurrent/racing dimension in the artifact to evade.

authority_boundary.proves: over a target repo working tree, detect_ci reports ci_present=false (a finding) exactly when .github/workflows/ contains no top-level file whose suffix is .yml or .yaml, and ci_present=true otherwise; a missing-CI finding is derived mechanically as (0 if report.ci_present else 1)
authority_boundary.artifact: a target repo working tree (its .github/workflows/ directory and language-stack marker files: go.mod / package.json / pyproject.toml / setup.py / requirements.txt)
assumptions: CI presence is a GitHub Actions notion only: workflow files under .github/workflows/ with a .yml/.yaml suffix. A repo whose CI lives in another system (GitLab CI, CircleCI, Jenkins) is out of scope and would be reported ci_present=false

the check is PRESENCE-only: it never reads workflow CONTENT, so a syntactically-present but no-op workflow (a valid-extension file that runs no build/test) is accepted as ci_present=true — a documented under-sampling boundary, not a bug

wf_dir.iterdir() is non-recursive: only top-level files directly under .github/workflows/ are considered; a workflow nested in a subdirectory is not a valid GitHub Actions workflow and is correctly not counted
the suffix comparison p.suffix in (".yml", ".yaml") is exact; the detector does not require a specific filename (any .yml/.yaml is accepted)

seeded_defect_trials.injected: the only workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml) is deleted — the textbook 'no CI on main' state; detect_ci returns ci_present=false

.github/workflows/ exists but the ci.yml is replaced with a non-workflow README.md (no .yml/.yaml suffix). The directory is present but holds no workflow — still MISSING by the detector's own definition, so it fires

the workflow is renamed ci.yml -> deploy-then-test.yaml (a real, valid, differently-named workflow). The detector requires SOME .yml/.yaml under .github/workflows/, not a specific filename, so a legitimately-named workflow correctly does NOT trip a false 'missing CI' finding

.github/workflows/ci.yml is present with a valid suffix but its CONTENT is a no-op workflow that runs no build or tests. detect_ci is presence+extension only and never reads content, so it accepts it as ci_present=true — a documented under-sampling boundary (the gate proves a workflow file EXISTS, not that it runs a real build/test)
